William Lloyd Garrison

1805 — 1879

Journalist. Abolitionist. Editor of The Liberator for 35 years. He demanded the immediate end of slavery and refused to be silent — or to compromise.

Explore Research View Timeline
William LloydGarrison

Reform Research Project · Joshua Curtis · US History I · Shrewsbury High School · 2026

01 · Biography 02 · Context 03 · Obstacles 04 · Action Plan 05 · Take Action 06 · Results 07 · Today 08 · Reflection

Section 01

Background & Biography

William Lloyd Garrison was born on December 10, 1805 in Newburyport, Massachusetts. During his lifetime he was an American journalistic crusader who fought against racial injustice through the power of the written word. He published the abolitionist newspaper The Liberator for 35 years, while also helping lead the successful campaign to end slavery in the United States.

His father was a sailor and merchant who abandoned the family when Garrison was just 3 years old, after a U.S.-British trade dispute shut down the port and cost him his job. Garrison and his brother sold molasses candy and clams to help the family survive. At age 10 he became a cobbler's apprentice, and by age 13 he was a printer's apprentice at the Newburyport Herald, where he also contributed essays.

In 1826 he started his own country paper, but it quickly failed. He moved to Boston in 1829 to serve as co-editor of the National Philanthropist, and later became co-editor of the Genius of Universal Emancipation alongside abolitionist Benjamin Lundy. He was briefly jailed for publicly attacking a Newburyport merchant involved in the slave trade. After his release, he founded The Liberator on January 1, 1831. His childhood atmosphere of declining New England federalism and Christian values motivated his entry into the abolition movement. He joined the cause in his mid-20s and served as editor of the National Philanthropist and Journal of the Times before becoming coeditor of the Genius of Universal Emancipation.

"A greater revolution in public sentiment was to be effected in the free States."

William Lloyd Garrison · The Liberator, Vol. 1, No. 1 · January 1, 1831

William Lloyd Garrison · Formal Portrait · c. 1870s

Section 02

Historical Context

Garrison came of age during one of the most morally divided periods in American history. During the early 1800s, slavery was widely accepted — especially in the South — and deeply tied to the cotton economy. Garrison grew up in a climate of declining New England federalism and Christian values, both of which motivated his entry into the abolition movement.

When Garrison began his career, resentment of abolitionists was strong in both the North and South. Southerners exaggerated abolitionist influence in the North, while Northerners believed abolitionists were stirring up trouble. In 1837, in the wake of a financial panic and the failure of abolitionist campaigns to gain support in the North, the movement faced serious new obstacles.

The Liberator — Garrison's abolitionist newspaper, published 1831–1865

Gradualism vs. Immediate Emancipation

Before Garrison, most abolitionists — including his mentor Benjamin Lundy — supported a gradual approach: slowly phasing out slavery over decades. Garrison broke sharply from this. In the very first issue of The Liberator, he made clear he would not wait, would not moderate his language, and would not compromise. Slavery was wrong, and it had to end now.

The Political Climate

The American political system was, in many ways, built to protect slavery. Garrison understood this early on, which is why he initially refused to work within that system at all — refusing to vote, refusing to join political parties, even calling the Constitution a "covenant with death" because of its protections for slaveholders. He believed the only real path to change was shifting what Americans believed — and letting the laws follow.

Section 03

Obstacles Faced

External Opposition
Violence and Mob Threats

In 1835, Garrison and some of his followers barely escaped a lynching at the hands of an angry mob in Boston. Earlier in his life, he was jailed for publicly calling out a merchant involved in the slave trade. Despite the danger, he refused to back down.

Resentment in Both Regions

Resentment of abolitionists was strong in both the North and South. Southerners exaggerated abolitionist influence in the North, while many Northerners believed abolitionists were stirring up unnecessary trouble.

Hostile Public Opinion

When Garrison started The Liberator in 1831, most Americans — even in free states — had made a kind of peace with the existence of slavery. His call for immediate emancipation struck many as unrealistic and extreme.

Internal Challenges
Splits Within the Movement

As Garrison began embracing more reform causes — including women's rights — many followers stopped supporting him. His strong beliefs also caused disagreement within the abolitionist movement, especially because he opposed gradual emancipation and criticized the Constitution.

Illness and Financial Troubles

In the years leading up to the Civil War, poor health and financial trouble kept Garrison from playing the active role he had in earlier decades, limiting him at a critical moment in history.

Garrison being mobbed by anti-abolitionists in Boston · 1835

Section 04

Action Plan

Specific Goal

Garrison's main goal was the immediate abolition of slavery. The Britannica document makes this clear by explaining that he rejected gradual emancipation and instead demanded that slavery should end right away. The Liberator was created to push this goal — everything he did was focused on ending slavery as quickly as possible.

Target Audience

Three Key Groups

Northern Americans in the free states — people who were either ignoring slavery or accepting slow change. Garrison believed that even people in free states needed to be confronted with the reality of what was happening in the country.
The broader American public — According to the article, Garrison wanted to spread his message to the American people, especially those in the North. He believed his articles could help change the public eye and create more followers passionate about ending slavery.
Political and reform causes — He also aimed at reforming other issues of the time including women's rights and imprisonment for debt, linking him to a broader network of 19th-century reformers.

Strategies Planned

Garrison planned on writing as the main strategy to persuade Americans. The Britannica lists many examples of his writing that were used to help end slavery. Other strategies included creating societies and public protest.

An abolitionist speaker addresses a public crowd · 19th century engraving

Section 05

Take Action

Actions Taken

Garrison published and spread many abolitionist ideas through newspapers and organizations that helped him reach the American people. His work such as The Liberator and other newspapers showed that he consistently used media to reach people and promote change. He also spoke publicly at anti-slavery events, having already been traveling and speaking before the newspaper was even founded.

As the Civil War drew near, Garrison condemned the Kansas-Nebraska Act and damned the Dred Scott decision. He supported Abraham Lincoln faithfully in 1863 and welcomed the Emancipation Proclamation. He also regularly supported the Republican Party. When slavery was finally abolished in 1865, he resigned from the American Anti-Slavery Society and published the final issue of The Liberator — declaring: "My vocation as an abolitionist is ended."

Strategies Used

01

The Liberator — Abolitionist Newspaper

Founded January 1, 1831 and published weekly for 35 years. Garrison used this newspaper to argue against slavery and demand immediate emancipation, writing direct statements to persuade readers that slavery was morally wrong. It became one of the most influential abolitionist publications in American history and helped persuade many people to join the cause.

02

American Anti-Slavery Society

Garrison helped organize the American Anti-Slavery Society in 1833, bringing abolitionists together to organize petitions, hold public lectures, and distribute pamphlets. He also helped organize the American Colonization Society and later formed the Liberty Party. At the 1840 World Anti-Slavery Convention in London, he refused to participate because women were excluded.

03

Public Speaking and Lecturing

Garrison consistently used public speaking to influence people. He traveled and gave lectures even before The Liberator was founded. He continued speaking at public events throughout his career, even when abolitionists faced physical violence and hostility from crowds.

04

Moral Suasion — Changing Hearts, Not Laws

In the beginning, Garrison believed that the only way to achieve the emancipation of slavery was by changing public opinion. He used moral arguments to convince people that slavery was wrong. Toward the end of the Civil War, he shifted and directly supported Lincoln and Republican policies, showing how his strategy evolved over time.

Alliances Formed

Benjamin Lundy: Garrison worked with Benjamin Lundy on an anti-slavery newspaper that he consistently used to influence people. Lundy was his first major abolitionist mentor, though the two eventually disagreed — Lundy favored gradual emancipation while Garrison demanded immediate action.

Frederick Douglass: Garrison helped bring Douglass into the public spotlight as an abolitionist speaker. They worked closely for years before splitting — Douglass eventually concluded that political action was necessary, while Garrison held firm to moral suasion alone.

Isaac Knapp: The Liberator was published with Isaac Knapp, who co-founded the paper with Garrison in 1831. Knapp handled the printing and business side that kept the paper going in its early years.

Legislation Supported

In the beginning, Garrison believed that the only way to end slavery was by changing public opinion — he did not focus on supporting specific laws. He believed that the political system was connected to slavery and laws would eventually change once people's beliefs changed. However, toward the end of the Civil War, he condemned the Kansas-Nebraska Act, denounced the Dred Scott decision, supported Abraham Lincoln faithfully in 1863, and welcomed the Emancipation Proclamation. His final act was accepting the ratification of the 13th Amendment as the fulfillment of everything he had worked toward.

Section 06

Results & Impact

35
Years Publishing The Liberator
1865
Slavery Finally Abolished
13th
Amendment — His Life's Goal

The Liberator became one of the crucial and influential newspapers to persuade people that slavery was morally wrong. Garrison's article was very successful and helped persuade many people to join the abolitionists movement. Through his writing, organizing, and public speaking, Garrison helped bring the nation's attention to the moral evil of slavery.

However, his influence began to wane as his radicalism reached its peak. While soon resigning from the American Anti-Slavery Society and publishing the last The Liberator, he announced: "My vocation as an abolitionist is ended." Post-Civil War, he didn't have much of an influence due to illness and financial trouble. But his work had a long-lasting impact that helped emancipate slaves.

Ultimately, slavery was abolished in 1865 with the ratification of the 13th Amendment. This was exactly the goal Garrison had fought for since January 1, 1831. Even after facing mobs, jail, financial hardship, and years of being dismissed as a radical, he had lived to see his goal become the law of the land.

Growth of Northern Anti-Slavery SentimentMajor
Years The Liberator Published35 yrs
Impact on National DebateFoundational
Goal of Abolition AchievedComplete

William Lloyd Garrison, later in life

Section 10

Connection to Today

Garrison's work did not just matter in the 1800s — the way he apporached activism still shows up in movements today. He believed that moral arguement, media, and public pressure could shift what society was willing to accept. That idea never really went away.

The Black Lives Matter movement is one of the clearest modern connections. Like Garrison, BLM used public protest, social media, and moral persuasion to force the country to confront racial injustice. Garrison published The Liberator to change public opinion; BLM used Instagram, Twitter, and street marches to do the same thing 150 years later. Both movements faced people who said they were "too radical" or "stirring up trouble" — the same critisisms Garrison heard his whole life.

Today, a statue of Garrison stands on Commonwealth Avenue in Boston — just a few miles from where he was once chased by a mob in 1835. That statue is a reminder that the people society calls extremists in one generation are often the ones it puts on pedestals in the next.

Garrison Statue · Commonwealth Avenue, Boston

Garrison statue on Commonwealth Avenue, Boston — near where he was attacked by a mob in 1835

Garrison spent his whole career being called dangerous, radical, and reckless. He was jailed, chased by mobs, and dismissed by most Americans as an extremist. Now his statue stands in one of Boston's most prestigious boulevards. That's not a coincidence — its the same pattern Garrison himself predicted. He believed moral truth would eventually win out, even if it took generations.

His approach — using media, building coalitions, and refusing to compromise on core principles — is a blueprint that reformers still follow today.

Section 11

Reflection

Essential Question

"How effective was William Lloyd Garrison in bringing about change in nineteenth century society?"

Your thesis statementWrite your argument here. A good thesis takes a clear position and gives reasons. Example: "William Lloyd Garrison was highly effective in bringing about change because he kept slavery in the national conversation for 35 years, built the infrastructure of the abolitionist movement, and lived to see his goal — the abolition of slavery — become law."
Were they successful? In what specific ways?Name concrete outcomes tied to Garrison's activism — the growth of the anti-slavery movement, the founding of the AASS, the influence of The Liberator on public opinion in the North, and ultimately the 13th Amendment abolishing slavery in 1865. Name any specific legislation or action that took place as a result of his activism.
What lasting impact did he have on American society?How is Garrison remembered today? What did he change that stayed changed? Did his work go beyond slavery — did his support for women's rights and other causes leave a lasting mark too?
What would you have done differently?If you were Garrison, what strategy would you have changed? Would you have worked within the political system earlier? Built different alliances? Use what you know from your research to think through what might have worked better.

Did You Know?

Fun Facts

Surprising details about William Lloyd Garrison's extraordinary life.

Jailed Before The Liberator Even Existed

Before starting his famous newspaper, Garrison was thrown in jail for publicly attacking a Newburyport merchant who was engaged in the slave trade. It was one of his first major acts of bold public protest — and it only made him more determined.

He Grew Up Selling Candy on the Street

After his father abandoned the family, young Garrison and his brother sold molasses candy and clams to help his mother make ends meet. He worked as a cobbler's apprentice at age 10 before finding his way into journalism.

He Called the Constitution Evil

Garrison publicly called the U.S. Constitution a "covenant with death" because it protected slavery. He went so far as to burn a copy of it in public — one of the most radical acts of protest in the antebellum period.

He Refused to Vote for Over 30 Years

Garrison believed that working inside a political system built on slavery made you complicit in it. He did not vote or endorse candidates for most of his adult life — only reversing this when the Civil War made it impossible to stay out of politics.

He Walked Out of an Anti-Slavery Convention

At the 1840 World Anti-Slavery Convention in London, women delegates were not allowed to participate. Garrison refused to take part in protest — a move that helped link abolitionism to women's rights.

His Newspaper Never Missed a Week

The Liberator ran every single week from January 1, 1831 until December 29, 1865 — the same year the 13th Amendment was ratified. Garrison wrote the final issue himself and shut it down, declaring his mission complete.

He and Frederick Douglass Fell Out

Garrison helped make Douglass famous as an abolitionist speaker, but the two had a serious break. Douglass decided political action was necessary to end slavery; Garrison disagreed. Their split reflected a deep divide about how change actually happens.

"My Vocation as an Abolitionist Is Ended"

When the 13th Amendment abolished slavery in 1865, Garrison published the final issue of The Liberator and resigned from the American Anti-Slavery Society. He had spent 35 years working toward that single moment — and he lived to see it become law.

A Life in History

Timeline of Key Events

The most important moments in William Lloyd Garrison's life and career. Tap any event to expand.

1805
Born in Newburyport, Massachusetts
Born December 10, 1805. His father abandoned the family when he was 3 after a trade dispute shut down the port. He and his brother sold candy and clams to survive. (Sources: Britannica School, YouTube Biography)
1818
Becomes a Printer's Apprentice
By 13 he was a printer's apprentice at the Newburyport Herald. In 1826 he tried starting his own paper, which failed. He moved to Boston in 1829 to co-edit the National Philanthropist. (Sources: ABC-CLIO, YouTube Biography)
1829
Works with Benjamin Lundy on Anti-Slavery Paper
Co-edited the Genius of Universal Emancipation with Lundy. Lundy favored gradual emancipation; Garrison pushed for immediate action. Garrison was jailed for attacking a Newburyport merchant in the slave trade. (Sources: Britannica School, ABC-CLIO)
1831
Founds The Liberator — January 1st
Founded The Liberator on January 1, 1831, co-published with Isaac Knapp. Using bold, uncompromising language he demanded immediate emancipation and declared he would not be silent. It ran every week for 35 years. (Primary Source: The Liberator, Vol. 1 No. 1)
1833
Co-Founds American Anti-Slavery Society
Helped organize the AASS in 1833, bringing abolitionists together to organize petitions, hold public lectures, and distribute pamphlets. He also helped organize the American Colonization Society and the Liberty Party. (Sources: Britannica School, ABC-CLIO)
1835
Attacked by an Angry Mob in Boston
Garrison and followers barely escaped a lynching by an angry mob in Boston. Garrison and some of his followers barely escaped, showing that resentment of abolitionists was strong even in the North. He refused to back down. (Source: ABC-CLIO)
1840
Refuses to Join World Anti-Slavery Convention
At the World Anti-Slavery Convention in London, Garrison refused to participate because women were excluded. This showed his commitment to gender equality alongside racial equality. (Source: ABC-CLIO)
1863
Supports Lincoln and the Emancipation Proclamation
Condemned the Kansas-Nebraska Act and the Dred Scott decision. He faithfully supported Abraham Lincoln in 1863 and welcomed the Emancipation Proclamation. He also regularly supported the Republican Party during the war years. (Source: Britannica School)
1865
"My vocation as an abolitionist is ended."
With the 13th Amendment ratified and slavery abolished, Garrison resigned from the AASS and published The Liberator's final issue. His 35-year campaign had succeeded. (Sources: Britannica School, ABC-CLIO)
1879
Death and Lasting Legacy
Garrison died in 1879. He is remembered as one of the most influential voices in the abolitionist movement — a journalist who spent 35 years demanding the immediate end of slavery and lived to see it happen. (Sources: Britannica School, ABC-CLIO)

Photo Gallery

Portraits, engravings, and historical images from Garrison's life and the abolitionist movement. Tap any image to view full size.

William Lloyd Garrison
Oil Portrait · c. 1850s

One of the most well-known portraits of Garrison, painted during the height of his influence as editor of The Liberator. He founded the paper on January 1, 1831 and published it every week for 35 years until slavery was abolished.

Formal Portrait
Photograph · c. 1870s
Garrison Later in Life
Photograph · Post-War Era
The Boston Mob Attack
Historical Engraving · October 1835

In October 1835, an anti-abolitionist mob dragged Garrison through the streets of Boston with a rope around him. He was taken into protective custody at Leverett Street Jail. He came out refusing to change a word he had written.

The Liberator — Newspaper Masthead
Published every week 1831–1865 · Boston, Massachusetts
Garrison Delivering a Public Address
19th Century Engraving · Reform Era

Garrison was a prolific public speaker long before The Liberator was founded. He traveled giving lectures about the moral evil of slavery, believing that face-to-face argument was one of the most powerful tools for changing minds.

The Researcher

About the Author

JC
Joshua Curtis
Sophomore · US History I · Shrewsbury High School · 2026

I'm a sophomore at Shrewsbury High School. Outside of school I love spending time with my dogs and playing golf. I chose William Lloyd Garrison for this project because his story of using writing and persistence to fight for what he believed in — even when almost everyone opposed him — stood out to me. This project is for Mr. Brad's class.

About This Project

This website was created as a USI Unit 5 Reform Research Project exploring William Lloyd Garrison's role in the 19th-century abolitionist movement. It covers his biography, the historical context of slavery in America before the Civil War, the obstacles he faced, and the actions he took to create change in American society.

Sources Used

Encyclopedia: Britannica School — "William Lloyd Garrison"

Database: McGuire, William, and Leslie Wheeler. "William Lloyd Garrison." American Government, ABC-CLIO, 2026.

Primary Source: Garrison, William Lloyd. The Liberator, Vol. 1, No. 1. January 1, 1831.

Video: Becker, A. Biography: William Lloyd Garrison. YouTube, 2018.
